setup: Your AI coding assistant has a 12% hallucination rate on TypeScript questions (verified by your QA team). Your largest enterprise customer's annual review is in 2 weeks. What do you ship first?

choices.label: Ship a 'verify before using' warning banner above every answer
Tune the system prompt to add 'be conservative when uncertain'
Add RAG over the official TypeScript docs as grounding
Build an eval pipeline and a rollback gate before changing anything else

reasoning: Without an eval you can't know if A, B, or C moved the needle — you'll be flying blind in front of the customer. D is the foundation: define ground truth, score current behaviour, then iterate on B/C with measurement. A is a band-aid that erodes trust; B is unmeasurable; C helps but you can't prove it without D.

setup: Your LLM-powered support chat has 4.2 s p95 latency. Drop-off after the first message is 38%, vs 11% for the legacy keyword-based bot. Engineering says GPT-4 is the floor on quality.

choices.label: Stream tokens to the UI so users see characters as they generate
Switch to a smaller model (Haiku / GPT-4o-mini) for the first response
Cache common queries (top 100 intents)
Cut the system prompt from 4k tokens to 800 by trimming examples

reasoning: Streaming is the cheapest, lowest-risk perceived-latency win — users see content in under 500 ms and stay engaged even if total response takes 5 s. B/C/D are real improvements but each carries quality risk and shipping cost. A doesn't change the model or prompt; it changes the UX. Ship A this sprint, evaluate B/C/D with an eval pipeline.

setup: Engineering proposes swapping the primary LLM from GPT-4 to Claude Sonnet to cut inference cost by 38%. Their benchmark on 200 internal prompts shows quality is 'roughly the same'.

choices.label: Approve — the cost savings are material and 200 prompts is a decent sample
Reject — you can't risk a model swap on a benchmark of 200
Ship a percentage rollout (5 → 25 → 100) gated on production-eval scores + user-feedback drop
Run both models in shadow for a week and compare on the production traffic distribution

reasoning: A is reckless — 200 prompts can't capture the long tail of real production behaviour. B over-indexes on caution and leaves the cost win on the table. D is good for measurement but takes a week of extra compute and doesn't actually ship. C does both: real production data, gated rollout, automatic rollback. Most senior PM answer.

setup: A user posts a screenshot on Twitter showing your AI cooking-assistant told them to 'use bleach to deglaze the pan'. 200k+ likes in 6 hours. Your CEO is texting. What's your move in the next 60 minutes?

choices.label: Roll back the model to the previous version immediately
Add a hardcoded filter blocking 'bleach' as a system-prompt rule, ship in 10 minutes
Public statement, then form a working group to study the issue
Investigate: pull the trace, confirm the screenshot is real, identify whether it's a prompt-injection or model failure, then decide between rollback and a targeted fix

reasoning: Speed is real but you cannot fix what you don't understand. The first 60 minutes is for evidence, not action. The screenshot might be doctored, might be prompt-injected, might be a known edge case — each implies a different response. A is correct ONLY if D's investigation confirms a model regression. B is fast but reactive; you'll be playing whack-a-mole. C delays without acting. D is the senior-PM move: triage with evidence, then act.

setup: Your B2B customer-support AI scores 30% accuracy on billing-and-permissions edge cases. The eng lead proposes fine-tuning a smaller model on 5,000 labelled past tickets. The marketing PM proposes RAG over your help docs. You have 6 weeks before the next release. Billing policy changed twice in the last quarter.

choices.label: Fine-tune — 5k labelled tickets is the highest-quality training signal you'll ever have
RAG over help docs — faster to ship, updatable as policy evolves
Ship RAG first (2 weeks), measure, then decide if fine-tuning adds enough lift to justify retraining every time policy changes
Both in parallel — fine-tune for tone and ticket-structure, RAG for current policy

reasoning: Sequencing matters. A bakes stale billing rules into the weights — every policy change triggers a retrain. B is fast but you don't know if it'll be enough. D doubles cost and engineering scope before you've validated either lever. C is the senior move: ship the cheapest, fastest, updatable solution first, measure the gap, then decide whether the marginal gain of fine-tuning is worth the ongoing retraining tax. The 2026 default is 'RAG by default, fine-tune for stable patterns only'.

setup: You're shipping an AI moderation classifier for a kids' learning app. The model flags content as 'unsafe'. Legal wants zero unsafe content reaching kids (high recall on the unsafe class). Product wants minimal false flags blocking good lessons (high precision). You can ship one classifier threshold. Where do you set it?

choices.label: Optimise for recall — block aggressively; better to over-flag than let one unsafe item reach a child
Optimise for precision — false flags destroy teacher trust and the product dies
Two-tier system: high-recall classifier auto-blocks the obvious; borderline items go to human review within 60 seconds
Run two thresholds in shadow for a week and present P/R curves to legal + product before deciding

reasoning: Precision vs recall isn't a single dial in safety-critical domains — it's a system-design choice. A alone destroys product utility; B alone is legally untenable. D delays a shippable answer. C resolves the tension: machines handle the obvious cases at high recall (no kid sees unsafe content), humans absorb the ambiguous middle where precision matters most. This is how every serious moderation system (TikTok, Roblox, Khan Academy) is actually built. The PM signal: recognise that the question isn't 'pick one threshold' — it's 'design the workflow around the trade-off'.

setup: Your AI feature: 'plan and book a 3-day business trip from one prompt.' Eng lead proposes an autonomous agent that plans, books flights via API, books hotel, books cabs, sends calendar invites. The demo wow'd your CEO. Your eval shows 64% end-to-end success on 50 benchmark prompts — failures include wrong dates, double bookings, and one agent that burned $40 in API calls before being killed.

choices.label: Ship — 64% with a 'review before confirming' step is good enough for v1
Pivot to a workflow: model proposes a plan, user confirms each step, system executes. Lower wow, much higher reliability
Add a budget cap and a max-step limit to the agent; ship in 2 weeks
Hybrid: workflow for the deterministic booking APIs, agent for the genuinely open-ended itinerary-planning step

reasoning: Agents shine where the path is genuinely open-ended (creative planning, research). They fail where the path is well-structured — booking APIs are deterministic, so call them in a known order. D is the architectural answer: use the right tool for each sub-task. A under-weights the cost of double-booking a customer's flight; the trust damage outlasts any 'wow'. B over-corrects — you lose the genuine intelligence in plan-generation. C is a band-aid that doesn't fix the architecture. D is the senior move: decompose the problem, use agents where they earn their keep, use workflows everywhere else.

setup: Your AI feature ships next month. Finance gives you $0.04 per active-user per day max inference budget. Current architecture (GPT-4 + 6k system prompt + 2k RAG context) costs $0.18 per user per day. Quality is great. You have engineering for 3 weeks.

choices.label: Switch to GPT-4o-mini for everything — it'll fit the budget
Tier the models: GPT-4o-mini for 80% of queries (classification, simple Q&A), GPT-4 for the 20% flagged complex
Cut the system prompt by 4k tokens, cap RAG context at 1k, keep GPT-4
Cache the top 500 query patterns; route uncached traffic through a smaller model

reasoning: Single-model decisions miss the obvious: not all queries need the same compute. A torches quality across the board to hit budget. C breaks quality on the hard queries that justify your differentiation. D helps but won't get you from $0.18 → $0.04 alone unless your traffic is extremely peaked. B (model routing) is the production pattern most serious AI products converge on — cheap model handles the long tail of simple queries, premium model handles the hard ones. C and D become multipliers once B is in place. The PM signal: ask 'do all queries need the same model?' before optimising any single dimension.
